method_atom: A real-space, strong-heterogeneity renormalization procedure that iteratively decimates the locally dominant coupling or field in a network of interacting binary degrees of freedom with quenched random parameters, flowing toward a fixed point governed by asymptotically broad (infinitely wide) parameter distributions. From this flow one extracts universal critical exponents, the sample-to-sample distribution of pseudo-critical points characterized by shift and width exponents, order-parameter scaling near criticality, and anomalous (activated) dynamical scaling in the rare-region-dominated phases flanking the transition. The scheme is implemented efficiently enough to treat very large finite samples.
object_atom: The two-dimensional random transverse-field Ising model on a square lattice: quantum spins with a discrete-symmetry order parameter subject to quenched-random ferromagnetic couplings and random transverse fields, exhibiting an infinite-disorder critical point flanked by ordered and disordered Griffiths phases.
brief_reason: The iterative strong-disorder decimation/renormalization machinery, its infinite-disorder fixed point, and the associated distributional and activated-dynamical scaling analysis transfer to any strongly disordered interacting system with a discrete-symmetry order parameter, while the transverse-field Ising spins on a square lattice are the physics-specific object.
